content:
Match the following Column I and Column II

Column I
(Reactions)		 Column II
(Volume at STP)
A. $$\mathop {CaC{O_3}}\limits_{20\,g} \mathrel{\mathop{\kern0pt\longrightarrow}
\limits_{Decomposition}^\Delta } $$		 I. $$0.896 \mathrm{~L~CO}_2$$
B. $$\mathop C\limits_{4.8\,g} \mathrel{\mathop{\kern0pt\longrightarrow}
\limits_{Combustion}^{Excess\,{O_2}}} $$		 II. $$8.96 \mathrm{~L~CO}_2$$
C. $$\mathop {N{a_2}C{O_3}}\limits_{2.12\,g} \mathrel{\mathop{\kern0pt\longrightarrow}
\limits_{}^{Excess\,HCl}} $$		 III. $$4.48 \mathrm{~L~CO}_2$$
D. $$\mathop {CO}\limits_{1.12\,g} \mathrel{\mathop{\kern0pt\longrightarrow}
\limits_{Combustion}^{Excess\,{O_2}}} $$		 IV. $$0.448 \mathrm{~L~CO}_2$$

options.content: A - I, B - II, C - III, D - IV
A - III, B - II, C - IV, D - I
A - IV, B - III, C - II, D - I
A - I, B - II, C - IV, D - III

explanation:
The correct match is A-III, B-II, C-IV, D-I.

$$\text { A. } \underset{100 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{CaCO}_3} \xrightarrow[\text { Decomposition }]{\Delta} \mathrm{CaO}+\underset{22.4 \mathrm{~L}}{\mathrm{CO}_2}$$

$$\because 100 \mathrm{~g}$$ of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$ on decomposition gives

$$=22.4 \mathrm{~L}^2 \text { of } \mathrm{CO}_2$$

$$\therefore 20 \mathrm{~g}$$ of $$\mathrm{CaCO}_3$$ on decomposition will give

$$=\frac{22.4 \times 20}{100} \mathrm{Lof}^2 \mathrm{CO}_2=4.48 \mathrm{LCO}_2$$

B. $$\mathrm{\mathop C\limits_{12\,g} \mathrel{\mathop{\kern0pt\longrightarrow} \limits_{Combustion}^{Excess\,{O_2}}} \mathop {C{O_2}}\limits_{22.4\,L}}$$

$$12 \mathrm{~g}$$ of carbon on combustion gives

$$=22.4 \mathrm{~L} \text { of } \mathrm{CO}_2=8.96 \mathrm{~LCO}_2$$

C. $$\underset{106 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3} \xrightarrow{\text { Excess HCl}} 2 \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\underset{22.4 \mathrm{~L}}{\mathrm{CO}_2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad 106 \mathrm{~g} \text { of } \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 \text { gives }=22.4 \mathrm{~L} \text { of } \mathrm{CO}_2 \\ & \therefore \quad 2.12 \mathrm{~g} \text { of } \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 \text { will give } \\ & \quad=\frac{22.4 \times 2.12}{106} \mathrm{~L} \text { of } \mathrm{CO}_2=0.448 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{CO}_2 \end{aligned}$$

D. $$2 \mathrm{CO} \xrightarrow[\text { Combustion }]{\text { Excess }} 2 \mathrm{CO}_2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & 2(12+16) \mathrm{g} \quad 2 \times 22.4 \mathrm{~L} \\ & 56 \mathrm{~g} \end{aligned}$$

$$56 \mathrm{~g}$$ of carbon monoxide on combustion gives $$=2 \times 22.4 \mathrm{~L}$$ of $$\mathrm{CO}_2$$

$$1.12 \mathrm{~g}$$ of carbon monoxide on combustion will give $$=\frac{2 \times 22.4 \times 1.12}{56} \mathrm{L~of~CO}_2=0.896 \mathrm{~L~CO}_2$$

content: Which of the following is an intensive property?
explanation: The properties of the system whose value is independent of the amount of substance present in the system are called intensive properties e.g. temperature. Whereas the properties of the system whose value is depends upon the amount of substance present in the system is called extensive property. e.g. Mass, volume, enthalpy.

content: Which one of the following concentration terms is/are independent of temperature?
explanation: Mole fraction and molality does not involve volume therefore, they are independent of temperature. Normality and molarity changes with temperature because they involve volumes.

content: What will be the molarity of solution when $$30 \mathrm{~mL}$$ of $$0.5 \mathrm{~M} \mathrm{~H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$$ is diluted to $$500 \mathrm{~mL}$$ ?
explanation:
Molarity of $$30 \mathrm{~mL}\left(V_1\right), \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4\left(M_1\right)=0.5 \mathrm{M}$$

Molarity of $$500 \mathrm{~mL}(\mathrm{V}_2)$$ solution $$=M_2$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { Now, } & M_1 V_1=M_2 V_2 \\ & 0.5 \times 30=M_2 \times 500 \\ & M_2=\frac{0.5 \times 30}{500} \\ \therefore \quad & M_2=0.03 \mathrm{~M} \end{array}$$

content: $$0.20 \mathrm{~g}$$ of an organic compound gave $$0.12 \mathrm{~g}$$ of $$\mathrm{AgBr}$$. By using Carius method, the percentage of bromine in the compound will be
explanation:
Given, mass of an organic compound $$ =0.20 \mathrm{~g}$$

Mass of $$\mathrm{AgBr}=0.12 \mathrm{~g}$$

Molecular mass of $$\mathrm{AgBr}=188 \mathrm{~gmol}^{-1}$$

$$188 \mathrm{~g}$$ of $$\mathrm{AgBr}$$ contains $$80 \mathrm{~g}$$ of bromine.

$$\therefore 0.12 \mathrm{~g}$$ of $$\mathrm{AgBr}$$ will contain $$=\frac{80}{188} \times 0.12$$

$$=0.05 \mathrm{~g}$$ of bromine

$$\therefore$$ Percentage of bromine $$=\frac{0.05}{0.20} \times 100=25 \%$$.
